sections.java: // Group values by key: the get-or-create dance Map<String, List<Order>> byCust = new HashMap<>(); for (Order o : orders) { byCust.computeIfAbsent(o.cust, k -> new ArrayList<>()).add(o); }
sections.python: # defaultdict supplies the empty list for you from collections import defaultdict by_cust: dict[str, list] = defaultdict(list) for o in orders: by_cust[o.cust].append(o) # key auto-created

sections.title: defaultdict Kills the get-or-create Pattern
sections.content: Pass a factory (list, set, int) and any missing key is initialized on first access — Python's computeIfAbsent, but ambient. defaultdict(int) makes a frequency counter; defaultdict(list) makes a multimap. No null checks, no putIfAbsent ceremony.

sections.java: // Count occurrences with streams Map<String, Long> counts = words.stream() .collect(Collectors.groupingBy( w -> w, Collectors.counting())); // Top-N needs more stream plumbing
sections.python: # Counter does it in one call from collections import Counter counts = Counter(words) counts["error"] # 0 if absent, never KeyError counts.most_common(3) # top-3 as (item, n) pairs

sections.title: namedtuple vs @dataclass vs deque
sections.content: namedtuple("Point", "x y") is a lightweight immutable record with named fields and tuple behavior — handy for quick fixed structures. For anything with methods or defaults, prefer @dataclass (Module 06). And deque is your ArrayDeque: O(1) append/appendleft/popleft, ideal for queues and sliding windows where a list's pop(0) would be O(n).

sections.question: What does `defaultdict(list)` give you that a plain dict does not?
options: Accessing a missing key auto-creates an empty list instead of raising KeyError
sections.explanation: A plain dict raises KeyError on a missing key. defaultdict(list) calls the factory (list) to create and store an empty list on first access — exactly the behavior you'd hand-roll with Java's computeIfAbsent(k, x -> new ArrayList<>()), but automatic.
